core_story.summary: Despite sig- nificant progress in image inpainting [29, 35, 57], video in- painting remains full of challenges due to the complexity of video scenarios. However, such meth- ods still face challenges: the warping operation introduces artifacts such as blur and distortion in the propagated pix- els, especially in scenarios characterized by significant ob- ject or camera motion and lighting changes [9]. Abstract In this paper, we present HomoGen, an enhanced video inpainting method based on homography propagation and diffusion models. In this paper, we propose an enhanced video inpainting framework, named HomoGen, which enables pixel prop- agation via Homography registration and designs a video diffusion model to Generate missing content. Extensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method qualitatively and quantitatively.

introduction_structure.main_message: Introduction Video inpainting aims to fill corrupted regions in a video with realistic and spatiotemporally coherent content [2].
introduction_structure.writing_strategy: Contrasts prior work and emphasizes unresolved weaknesses.
introduction_structure.evidence_text:
1. Introduction
Video inpainting aims to fill corrupted regions in a video
with realistic and spatiotemporally coherent content [2]. It
has diverse applications including object removal [7], video
restoration [43], autonomous driving [23], etc. Despite sig-
nificant progress in image inpainting [29, 35, 57], video in-
painting remains full of challenges due to the complexity
of video scenarios. The primary difficulty lies in identify-
ing spatiotemporal guidance for generating content within
corrupted regions, particularly in videos with diverse cam-
era movements and object motion. To tackle this, previous
work has explored various technologies, such as 3D convo-
lutions [4, 13] and attentions [21, 24, 58], to leverage spa-
tiotemporal context and preserve coherent across frames.
Recently, two-stage methods combining flow-based
propagation and generation networks have shown impres-

frames into corrupted regions via warping. For genera-
tion networks, powerful video transformers are employed to
generate remaining missing content. However, such meth-
ods still face challenges: the warping operation introduces
artifacts such as blur and distortion in the propagated pix-
els, especially in scenarios characterized by significant ob-
ject or camera motion and lighting changes [9]. As shown
in Fig. 1, the flow-based propagation method in the state-
of-the-art ProPainter [63] incorrectly warps a tree into the
road center in the first stage. Besides, the propagated pixels
are considered partial inpainting results to guide remaining
content generation. Such artifacts cause error accumulation
in video transformers, leading to unrealistic results [22, 59].

raw_sections.abstract:
Abstract
In this paper, we present HomoGen, an enhanced video
inpainting method based on homography propagation and
diffusion models. HomoGen leverages homography regis-
tration to propagate contextual pixels as priors for gener-
ating missing content in corrupted videos. Unlike previ-
ous flow-based propagation methods, which introduce local
distortions due to point-to-point optical flows, homography-
induced artifacts are typically global structural distortions
that preserve semantic integrity. To effectively utilize these
priors for generation, we employ a video diffusion model
that inherently prioritizes semantic information within the
priors over pixel-level details. A content-adaptive control
mechanism is proposed to scale and inject the priors into
intermediate video latents during iterative denoising. In
contrast to existing transformer-based networks that often
suffer from artifacts within priors, leading to error accumu-
lation and unrealistic results, our denoising diffusion net-
*This work was done when Ding Ding was an intern at MSRA.
†Corresponding author.
work can smooth out artifacts and ensure natural outputs.
Extensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of the
proposed method qualitatively and quantitatively.
raw_sections.introduction:
1. Introduction
Video inpainting aims to fill corrupted regions in a video
with realistic and spatiotemporally coherent content [2]. It
has diverse applications including object removal [7], video
restoration [43], autonomous driving [23], etc. Despite sig-
nificant progress in image inpainting [29, 35, 57], video in-
painting remains full of challenges due to the complexity
of video scenarios. The primary difficulty lies in identify-
ing spatiotemporal guidance for generating content within
corrupted regions, particularly in videos with diverse cam-
era movements and object motion. To tackle this, previous
work has explored various technologies, such as 3D convo-
lutions [4, 13] and attentions [21, 24, 58], to leverage spa-
tiotemporal context and preserve coherent across frames.
Recently, two-stage methods combining flow-based
propagation and generation networks have shown impres-
This CVPR paper is the Open Access version, provided by the Computer Vision Foundation.
Except for this watermark, it is identical to the accepted version;
the final published version of the proceedings is available on IEEE Xplore.
22953

sive performance [22, 59, 63]. In flow-based propagation,
optical flows are utilized to propagate pixels from adjacent
frames into corrupted regions via warping. For genera-
tion networks, powerful video transformers are employed to
generate remaining missing content. However, such meth-
ods still face challenges: the warping operation introduces
artifacts such as blur and distortion in the propagated pix-
els, especially in scenarios characterized by significant ob-
ject or camera motion and lighting changes [9]. As shown
in Fig. 1, the flow-based propagation method in the state-
of-the-art ProPainter [63] incorrectly warps a tree into the
road center in the first stage. Besides, the propagated pixels
are considered partial inpainting results to guide remaining
content generation. Such artifacts cause error accumulation
in video transformers, leading to unrealistic results [22, 59].
To mitigate artifacts in the propagated pixels, recent meth-
ods propose the flow consistency check to avoid inaccurate
flows [56]. However, the consistency check discards partial
flows, leaving the propagated pixels insufficient and seman-
tically incoherent. As shown in Fig. 1, a subset of road pix-
els is removed from the propagated content after the con-
sistency check. Therefore, video transformers struggle to
generate high-fidelity content without sufficient guidance.
Regarding the challenges in balancing retaining more
propagated pixels while avoiding artifacts, diffusion models
[12, 35] offer a promising solution as generation networks
in the two-stage pipeline. Diffusion models prioritize se-
mantic information within input conditions, such as stroke
paintings and unnatural images [25, 28, 61], over pixel-level
details. During denoising, diffusion models could generate
realistic content while filtering out noisy low-level features
from the conditions. Besides, homography registration for
pixel propagation is more compatible with diffusion mod-
els. Specifically, artifacts from homography differ from
those caused by flows. Flows capture point-to-point corre-
lations [44], and flow-induced artifacts often considerably
distort parts of objects in complex scenes, disrupting the se-
mantics of propagated content. In contrast, homography im-
poses projective geometric constraints based on the global
structure between frames [42], and homography-induced ar-
tifacts are typically structural distortions while preserving
semantic integrity. As shown in Fig. 1, in the first stage,
although several road pixels experience overall distortions,
the structural integrity of objects remains largely intact. In
the second stage, the video diffusion model generates more
natural content while filtering out the artifacts.
In this paper, we propose an enhanced video inpainting
framework, named HomoGen, which enables pixel prop-
agation via Homography registration and designs a video
diffusion model to Generate missing content. For pixel
propagation, we register paired frames via homography ma-
trices to explore valuable contextual pixels. After warping
the reference frame, the propagated pixels located in the
corrupted regions of the target frame are extracted as pri-
ors. We iterate the propagation process across all frames in
a bidirectional manner, where the reference frames are pro-
gressively updated with the propagated pixels at each step.
For content generation, we design a prior controlled video
diffusion model that captures the semantics within the bidi-
rectional priors. Built upon a text-guided image inpainting
model from Stable Diffusion, we integrate temporal layers
into it to construct a 3D network. To effectively utilize the
priors, we propose a content-adaptive control mechanism
that scales and injects the priors into the diffusion model.
Specifically, we design a lite ControlNet [61] to embed the
priors into multi-scale features that are then fed into the
3D U-Net via dense injection. Learned scalers adjust the
multi-scale features before injection, adapting to the spa-
tiotemporal context of priors and the sampling steps. Simul-
taneously, the denoising process filters out artifacts within
the priors during iterative sampling, thus preserving high-
fidelity results across inpainted regions and visible pixels.
Qualitative and quantitative experiment results demon-
strate the superiority of HomoGen both in video comple-
tion and object removal. Compared to the state-of-the-art
methods, HomoGen can generate more realistic content and
improve performance by 23.81% in VFID.
• A propagate-and-diffuse framework is proposed for video
inpainting, which leverages homography registration to
propagate contextual pixels as priors to control the gener-
ation of missing content in a denoising diffusion network.
• A content-adaptive control mechanism is designed to in-
ject prior information into the diffusion model. The dense
injection learns to adapt to the spatiotemporal context of
priors and the sampling steps, while artifacts within the
priors are filtered out during iterative denoising.
• Extensive experiments show that HomoGen surpasses the
state-of-the-art methods and generates more realistic con-
tent in video completion and object removal tasks.
raw_sections.method:
3. Methodology
HomoGen consists of two main components:Homography-
based Pixel Propagation (HPP) and Prior Controlled Video
Diffusion Model (PCVDM), as shown in Fig. 2. In HPP,
we estimate homography between paired frames for propa-
gating pixels as contextual priors. PCVDM then generates
missing content controlled by these priors.
raw_sections.conclusion:
5. Conclusion
Our work introduces a propagate-and-diffuse framework for
video inpainting, named HomoGen, which utilizes homog-
raphy registration to propagate contextual pixels for gener-
ating missing content in a video diffusion model. Experi-
ments not only show HomoGen’s superiority in generating
realistic content but prove the synergy between homography
registration and video diffusion models for inpainting.
Limitations. There are several potential limitations in this
study. For instance, generating identity-preserved content
in videos with scene alternations and efficiently process-
ing high-resolution videos remain challenges for both Ho-
moGen and previous methods. These issues warrant further
investigation and improvement in future work.
